analysis: Top-performing heuristics (notably #3–#6) share a clear structure: fast Pareto filtering to shrink N, cheap per-point proxies (box/box-volume, product to reference) to rank candidates, a small proxy-formed shortlist, and a CELF-style lazy greedy that caches marginal gains with timestamps to dramatically reduce exact hypervolume (HV) calls. They finish with bounded, first‑improvement or randomized swap local search to recover small improvements. Common efficiency pitfalls in weaker examples: recomputing HVs from scratch in inner loops (no caching), repeated array copies/vstacking, expensive quadratic nondomination checks without vectorization, and missing candidate caps (leading to unbounded HV evaluations). TPOSS (Heuristic 1) uses a population/metaheuristic that increases runtime without exploiting HV caching; GSI_LS (Heuristic 2) computes HV for every removal/addition on the full set, causing many redundant HV evaluations. Many implementations also create/tear down pygmo hypervolume objects repeatedly and perform try/except in hot loops, both avoidable.
experience: Design for minimal exact-HV calls: pareto-filter → precompute box-volume proxies → cap candidates to O(k) → use CELF lazy greedy with timestamped cached marginal gains → reuse arrays/incremental HV where possible → finish with tiny bounded swap search. Vectorize nondomination and avoid per-call object construction to save orders of magnitude in runtime.
current_reflection: Design for minimal exact-HV calls: pareto-filter → precompute box-volume proxies → cap candidates to O(k) → use CELF lazy greedy with timestamped cached marginal gains → reuse arrays/incremental HV where possible → finish with tiny bounded swap search. Vectorize nondomination and avoid per-call object construction to save orders of magnitude in runtime.
good_reflections: Prefer a hybrid constructive: filter dominated points, approximate contributions with vectorized Monte‑Carlo or fast tensor proxies to shortlist candidates, then apply exact HV only on the shortlist with cached/incremental updates and a small swap-based local search. Vectorize dominance precomputation, avoid rebuilding objects in loops, and keep all heavy work batched.

Next generation heuristics should: (1) pareto‑filter then shortlist by cheap proxy; (2) compute exact marginal HVC only on that shortlist using cached/incremental HV data structures; (3) vectorize or Monte‑Carlo only when needed; (4) cap HV evaluations and local search adaptively; and (5) reuse pygmo objects and add deterministic seeding and parallel shortlist evaluation.
bad_reflections: Prefer Pareto filter + tight proxy upper bounds + CELF lazy greedy with cached (timestamped) marginal gains, small O(k log n) candidate pools, reuse hypervolume objects or incremental HV updates, vectorize dominance filtering, and cap local search. This minimizes HV calls while keeping HV quality high.

Favor a Pareto filter, form a tiny proxy-ranked shortlist (O(max(k,200))), run CELF-style lazy greedy with cached marginal gains and strict HV-call caps, then apply a bounded first‑improvement swap search. Use vectorized distance/proxy computations, efficient nondominated sorting, and deterministic caps/seeding; parallelize independent HV evaluations. This yields high hypervolume with predictable, low runtime.

Favor a CELF lazy greedy with Pareto prefiltering and a box‑volume upper bound, limit candidates to O(max(k,200)), cache marginal gains with timestamps, and cap exact HV evaluations. Use small, budgeted swap refinements and avoid repeated array copies or full HV recomputations—this yields high HV for minimal extra runtime.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: Pareto filter, proxy upper bounds, CELF lazy greedy, cached marginal gains, incremental HV, vectorization, bounded swap search.
- Advice: Pareto‑filter then shortlist using cheap box/tensor/MC proxies (cap O(max(k,200))). Run CELF with timestamped cached marginals and incremental HV updates; finish with a tiny bounded swap local search. Batch and vectorize dominance/proxy computations; reuse objects/arrays.
- Avoid: Per-call object construction, full HV recomputation, unbounded candidate pools, nondeterministic shortcuts without seeding.
- Explanation: This minimizes exact HV evaluations while preserving solution quality and runtime predictability by focusing heavy work on a small, well-ranked candidate set and reusing cached/incremental results.
